questions.question: Explain the three lifecycle methods of a servlet with their invocation stages.
questions.hint: Remember the sequence: init → service → destroy
keyPoints: init() - called once when servlet is first loaded
service() - called for each client request
destroy() - called once when servlet is unloaded

questions.question: Differentiate between doGet() and doPost() methods in servlet programming.
questions.hint: Consider HTTP method characteristics and data transmission
keyPoints: POST passes data via request body (hidden)
GET is idempotent, POST is not

questions.question: Describe the purpose and usage of the init() method in servlets.
questions.hint: Think about what happens before first request
keyPoints: Called once when servlet is instantiated

questions.question: List and explain the three JSP lifecycle methods.
questions.hint: JSP translates to servlet; remember the underscore in _jspService
keyPoints: jspInit() - called once during JSP initialization
jspDestroy() - called when JSP is unloaded

questions.question: What is the role of the service() method in servlet processing?
questions.hint: It's the main dispatcher in HttpServlet
keyPoints: Entry point for all client requests
Dispatches to doGet/doPost based on request type

questions.question: Draw and explain the complete servlet lifecycle with a state diagram showing initialization, request handling, and destruction phases.
questions.hint: Include: Loaded → Instantiated → Initialized → Ready → Destroyed
keyPoints: Service thread created for each request
Single init, multiple service calls, single destroy

questions.question: Explain the JSP lifecycle in detail with translation, compilation, and execution phases.
questions.hint: Remember JSP has translation phase not present in pure servlets
keyPoints: JSP translated to servlet Java code
Servlet Java code compiled to class file
Life cycle differs from applet/servlet in compilation step

questions.question: Compare GET and POST methods in the context of servlet request handling, considering data transfer, security, and performance.
questions.hint: Consider both technical and practical aspects
keyPoints: GET: data in URL query string
POST: data in HTTP message body

questions.question: Discuss the key methods of HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse interfaces used in servlet development.
questions.hint: Remember to distinguish between request and response methods
keyPoints: Request for input, Response for output

questions.question: Design a servlet that handles both GET and POST requests, demonstrating proper method overriding and request forwarding.
questions.hint: Show unified handling pattern for both methods
keyPoints: Use doGet(req, resp) in doPost() or vice versa
Implement getParameter() to read form data

questions.question: Write a servlet to read parameters using getParameter() method and display them.
questions.hint: Remember to check for null values
keyPoints: Use request.getParameter() to read single parameter
Set content type before getting writer

questions.question: Given a scenario where a servlet receives 1000 requests per minute, calculate the number of times init(), service(), and destroy() methods will be invoked. Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Lifecycle methods have different invocation frequencies
keyPoints: init() called once - when servlet is first loaded
service() called 1000 times per minute (once per request)
destroy() called once (or zero times if server doesn't shut down)
Servlet instance reused for multiple requests

questions.question: A web application has 3 JSP pages. If each page receives 50 requests per hour, calculate the total number of _jspService() invocations in 1 hour. Also determine jspInit() calls.
questions.hint: Each JSP is a separate servlet after translation
keyPoints: Each JSP translates to separate servlet
Each servlet instantiated once → jspInit() = 3 times
jspDestroy() called when container unloads JSPs

questions.question: If a servlet's init() takes 100ms to execute and service() takes 10ms per request, calculate total execution time for 100 requests arriving sequentially.
questions.hint: Sequential means one after another; concurrency changes calculation
keyPoints: destroy() negligible if no cleanup needed
Note: In real scenario, requests may be concurrent

questions.question: Explain the purpose and working of cookies in servlet API with relevant methods.
questions.hint: Remember cookies are added to response, read from request
keyPoints: Can set path, domain, secure flags
